Yeah, it's been like, I think it's just been this very organic journey.
It's been fun.

It was like, so at Deepgram, when I was there, I think like throughout my career, had just built a lot of expertise in how to grow organically, you know, and every step of the way was kind of the same thing where companies are spending so much money and they're like, we need more people, right, to know about us.
And it's like, how do we do it?
And it's like, well, outbound and paid channels will only get you so far.
They're good, but they're good for certain things, but they're diminishing returns.
Right.

And so the only way to grow organically is to build compounding asset, which I'm thinking of it as like content as like the knowledge that you share and how you teach your customers and how you teach your audience, how you become a trusted advisor and all of that.
Right.
But doing content well is just really, really hard.
And I had done this in every single company, but it was always the kind of the same recipe.
Right.
Which was like you hire an editor, you hire writers, you produce content, you need designers, you need SEO, you need this and that.
And then it's like, okay, now I produced a hundred pieces of content.
I'm to keep that fresh all the time.
And, you know, and it's like, and now how do I distribute this content?
How do I, you know, create offers to drive this audience, you know, to become subscribers or leads or, you know.
And so then at DeepGram, I essentially started taking all my processes, everything that I had taught my teams and essentially just sealing them into LLM steps, right?
Like AI workflows and just thinking through like playing thousands of hours just for fun of like, okay, how would I, how would I create something?
How would I research a topic?
How would I plan an outline?
How do I execute that outline?
It's just distilling everything.
And then it turns out it worked.
And I put like editors in the loop with 24 XR traffic really quickly.
And then we just had this kind of moment where I started sharing more and more on LinkedIn and people were like, can you please do a workshop on this?
I did.
And then we had about 170 people that pay 500 bucks to come to it.
And then from there, we just started getting customers like nonstop without even trying.
And these are like all like awesome legit brands, you know, and but then pretty quickly as we started to scale and I was still CMO, I like, wait, I got to quit my job because this is crazy.
And then but then really quickly, we realized the process of just distilling expertise into workflows and figuring out where the human intervention should be.
And then coupling that with strategy and execution is like really, really hard.
Right.
And, and then there's just no way that you can just go build a platform for this.

Without actually delivering the services yourself and learning from that process and building a platform that enables you to deliver the service and then over time figuring out like how do you expose more and more if anything, right?
And then it just so happens that any category in the world services 50 times bigger than software for that category and it just so happens that content was where we had domain expertise as well.

So it just was a like logical like first service met that we placed and yeah and then now underneath the hood what we build us think of it as like an AI coding agent that builds workflows and a runtime layer that runs those workflows like last last month we did about like 500,000 runs of different workflows, you know, and then now we have like our task hub and a learn engine.
So if you think about the learning and it's like workflows are, actually I can show you some stuff.
Yeah, that'd be awesome.
Yeah, just, you know.
It's like everyone's dream.

So take this one as, hey, we need to research topics.
A deep researcher is not good enough.
How do we build our own version of a deep researcher?

So this is like a workflow that, you know, validates an input, figures out what questions needs to be answered as part of researching this topic, and then generates all of this, and then does a bunch of parallel execution, and then combines all of this, and also stores it into a vector DB, and then figures out how to retrieve it later, right?
Like, and all of this is auto-documented because it's using our coding agent, you know, and then underneath the hood, this is like our runtime layer that's executing, like, just like right now.
These are all executions happening right now, you know, so like this is like creating an assignment right now as we speak, right, and doing all these processes.
These are all

And when do you publish this externally?
We do, but then that layer is, like, you have a managing editor, and then editors and writers.
That will use our workflows, and then in our workflows, there's, like, different places where there's interventions.
And then at the end of the day, there's also, like, a heavy, like, human pass to make sure, like, it's good, it's on brand.
Like, for example, like, abnormal security, obviously, like, their standards are going to be very different than this travel brand, for example, right?
But then what we started to realize is a lot of these workflows can be used for so many other things.
So, for example, bolt.new, you know, we...
We built a workflow to build a prompt generator engine that creates one-shot apps using Bolt itself.

Yeah, so right now, our customers are not...
Our users are actually the delivery team.

I think I met him, yeah.

Apple's blog for a while.
he was head of communications at Shopify after that.

And then you have teams in the pods that are the ones that are kind of doing the human interventions, right.

But then if you think about, like, I think we were just like, so unique in our knowledge in the sense that my co founder built, if this than that, like back in the day, so like massive distributed infrastructures, right.

And so we pretty much rebuild that level of infrastructure, you know, but then also just from my experience, scale and deep gram of just, I've just uniquely been in two companies back to back that not only were in AI, but literally had their own data labeling operations, train their own models, train their their own models.
And so I kind of saw like what data was valuable.
what I kind of realized is like all this frontier data that OpenAI and others are paying billions of dollars, they're all expert data.
Expert data is not about you take an expert and say, hey, pretend this scenario exists.
Go do this work.
Like what we're doing instead of saying, hey, we're signing up to do this service.
And then we're distilling that expertise in code, which is like essentially like a blueprint, almost like a config file, like a Terraform config file for knowledge work.
But then we're adding the human interventions.
But then the human interventions are the activities that are essentially reinforcement learning for that work to be done.
And all that activity is just like insanely valuable because, hey, in order to produce a piece of content, you need to have knowledge in that space.
And then you have all these interventions.
As you're doing that intervention, you can use your own models and your own workflows to give the right guidance to the people doing the intervention.
You can evaluate.
But then you can also use humans to evaluate the outputs and vice versa.
so there's kind of.
And so for us, the big picture is that it's building this canvas of what does it look like for complex, high-value work for AI and experts to interact?
And what we are hearing from all the companies is that they're not trying to just have just the really  work automated.

What they really want is take their best people, figuring out their thought processes, how they go about producing their best work products, and they want to, like, reproduce that at scale so that, you know, everyone else operates at 80% of their best person, but then their best person is augmented times 100, right?
And maybe those people that operate at 80% can get to 100 by learning their way of sharing, like, the recipe.
the thought processes and things like that, you know?
And I think, like, there's just this misconception, I think, with agents now where, like, we're a little bit contrarian in that, like, because we're...
Doing the work, and we're like, like, our workflows are getting way more complex, not simpler.

And like, there's no way an agent can learn from that without an expert saying, these are the hundred steps I go through.
And going through the iterations of doing it and having human interventions, learning from those interventions and things like that, you know?
But, and that's why we're building a task hub right now, which is kind of doing that orchestration between the human intervention layer or expert intervention, however you want to call it, you know?

that's really interesting.

Like, one second area that we built it for ourselves, and we're thinking of, like, potentially being our second endeavor is definitely on the recruiting side, because we get over 1,000 applicants a day already.

Yeah, so for us, it's still like an early hypothesis, but we're doing it for ourselves, and when we tell people what we're doing, they're like, wait, what?
Like, essentially, we built a workflow that it's...

Almost like you have recruiters in the loop, and then the workflow goes through, determines, like, and calibrates with you on expectations for the job, comes up with the right questions, and people submit those questions and answers, and then it kind of vets out a lot of people, but gives a lot of reasoning, and then you have, like, a recruiter in the loop, like, kind of, like, making sure everything is good, passing them to the next one.

The next stage is, like, async video interview, then you transcribe that, and pass that along, and then it goes into the assignment, which your assignment is, like, you know, there's a human involvement in an interview, but then, you know, vetting the assignment, it also helps as well.

So then it's, like, it's not going from, like, 100% automation, but it's going from hiring one person out of a pool of 10,000, go from, like, thousands of hours of work to, like, five live interviews, and maybe, like, you know, 20, 30 hours of a recruiter in the loop who is international and doesn't cost us that much.
So it's, like, the cost of hiring...
Incremental person is like so much and it makes sense for roles where it's like you're hiring in volume, you know, definitely roles where it's just like you need a high-end consultant.
It's probably doesn't make sense, you know, much because it's not about volume, right?
